items.q: Why does historical context matter when reading literature?
items.correct: Texts emerge from and respond to specific historical conditions — context illuminates references, assumptions and arguments that might otherwise be invisible.
items.misconception: Context just means writing about what the author had for breakfast.
items.random_wrong: Context is required because all texts are about history.
items.why: Students treat context as biographical trivia. Useful context is what the text engages with — politics, ideology, genre conventions — not personal detail of the author's life.

items.q: What did Romantic poets value in nature?
items.correct: Nature as a source of spiritual insight and emotional truth, accessed through imagination — not picturesque scenery for its own sake.
items.misconception: Romantic poets just liked to write about pretty landscapes.
items.random_wrong: Romantic poets believed nature was full of fairies and elves.
items.why: The Romantic engagement with nature was philosophical — it was a route to truth, the sublime and the self. Reducing it to 'pretty scenery' misses Wordsworth's epistemology entirely.

items.q: What is the difference between a theme and a symbol?
items.correct: A theme is an abstract idea or concern the text explores (e.g. mortality); a symbol is a concrete image that stands for something else (e.g. a skull).
items.misconception: Themes are just symbols repeated through the book.
items.random_wrong: A theme is the title of the book.
items.why: Students treat theme and symbol as the same thing. Themes are ideas, symbols are images. Skulls (symbol) can develop the theme (mortality), but they are not the same level of abstraction.

items.q: Who coined the term unreliable narrator in 1961?
items.why: It's Wayne Booth. “Northrop Frye” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which Marquez novel exemplifies magic realism?
items.misconception: Love in the Time of Cholera
items.why: Correct answer: One Hundred Years of Solitude. “Love in the Time of Cholera” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Marquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude published?
items.why: The real figure is 1967 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
